question_text:
# Smart Waste Sorting with CNN Architectures

A recycling company wants to automate waste sorting using AI. The system looks at an image of a waste item and classifies it into the correct category so it can be sent to the right recycling bin.

You are given a dataset of real-world waste photographs (resized to 32x32 RGB) across 10 categories. You will build a few CNN models, see how CNN architectures evolved over time, and compare them.

## Dataset

| Item | Details |
|------|---------|
| File | waste_images.npz |
| Image size | 32 x 32 x 3 (RGB) |
| Training images | 3200 |
| Validation images | 800 |
| Classes (10) | cardboard, glass, metal, paper, plastic, trash, organic, battery, clothes, electronics |

Labels are integers 0 to 9 in the class order listed above.

## Task 1: Data Loading

Build a custom PyTorch Dataset and create `train_loader` and `val_loader`. Scale pixel values to [0, 1] and use channel first format (N, 3, 32, 32).

## Task 2: Implement LeNet-5

Build the LeNet-5 architecture as `lenet_model`. Train it for 10 epochs with CrossEntropyLoss and the Adam optimizer, printing training loss, training accuracy, and validation accuracy each epoch.

## Task 3: Improve the CNN using AlexNet Ideas

Create `improved_model`, a version of LeNet-5 that uses `nn.ReLU` activations and at least one `nn.Dropout` layer. Train it for 10 epochs and compare its validation accuracy with LeNet-5.

## Task 4: Build a GoogLeNet-inspired CNN

Implement a custom Inception Module with four parallel branches, then concatenate their outputs along the channel dimension:

* 1x1 Convolution
* 1x1 followed by 3x3 Convolution
* 1x1 followed by 5x5 Convolution
* 3x3 MaxPooling followed by 1x1 Convolution

Use it to build `inception_model` and train it for 10 epochs with CrossEntropyLoss and the Adam optimizer, printing the same metrics each epoch.

## Task 5: Model Evaluation

Evaluate `lenet_model` and `inception_model` on the validation set. Show the validation accuracy, a confusion matrix for each model, and the predicted vs actual labels for any 5 validation images.

## Task 6: Compare the Models

Store the three validation accuracies in a dictionary named `model_results` and print them so the models can be compared.

## Expected Variables

| Variable | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| train_loader, val_loader | DataLoaders for the 3200 train and 800 validation images |
| lenet_model | Trained LeNet-5 |
| improved_model | LeNet-5 with ReLU and Dropout |
| inception_model | GoogLeNet-inspired CNN |
| model_results | Dict mapping the 3 model names to validation accuracy |

Use these exact variable names. All models take input of shape (N, 3, 32, 32) and output 10 class scores. Do not use plt.show() in your submission.
short_text: Smart Waste Sorting with CNN Architectures

test_cases.display_text: The solution should run without any errors
Training and validation DataLoaders should be created with 3200 and 800 samples respectively
lenet_model should follow the LeNet-5 design (at least 2 convolutional and 2 fully connected layers) and output scores for 10 classes
lenet_model accuracy on hidden test images should be at least 0.40 (train for the full 10 epochs)
improved_model should use nn.ReLU activations and include at least one nn.Dropout layer
improved_model accuracy on hidden test images should be at least 0.40 (train for the full 10 epochs)
inception_model should contain an Inception module with 1x1, 3x3, and 5x5 convolution branches and a max pooling branch, and output scores for 10 classes
inception_model accuracy on hidden test images should be at least 0.40 (train for the full 10 epochs)
A 10x10 confusion matrix computed on the full validation set (800 samples) should be present
model_results dictionary should map the 3 model names to their validation accuracies (floats between 0 and 1)
The solution should complete within 600 seconds
